coredata.dc:title: Carbon dots encapsulated zeolitic imidazolate framework-8 as an enhanced multi-antioxidant for efficient cytoprotection to HK-2 cells

coredata.dc:description: Excessive reactive oxygen species (ROS) can lead to the imbalance of antioxidant system in the body and cause oxidative damage to cells. It is imperative to rationally design nanomaterials with high catalytic activity and multiple antioxidant activities. Here, line peppers-derived carbon dots (CDs) is encapsulated into zeolitic imidazolate framework-8 (CDs@ZIF-8) to achieve enhanced antioxidant activities for improved protective effect on cells. This nanosystem has a broad spectrum of antioxidant properties, which can effectively remove a variety of intracellular ROS and protect cells from ROS-induced death and cytoskeleton damage. In addition, CDs@ZIF-8 can reduce malondialdehyde (MDA) level and increase the enzyme activities of superoxide dismutase (SOD) and glutathione peroxidase (GPx), as well as the level of glutathione (GSH) in human kidney proximal tubular epithelial cells (HK-2) cells. Mechanism studies demonstrated that CDs@ZIF-8 can up-regulate the expression of nuclear factor erythroid 2-related factor 2 (Nrf2) and heme oxygenase-1 (HO-1), allowing the regulation of antioxidant enzymes to further achieve antioxidant effect. Besides, CDs@ZIF-8 inhibited the secretion of proinflammatory cytokines. This work demonstrates that the constructed CDs@ZIF-8 with multi-antioxidant activity can act as a highly efficient intracellular ROS scavenger and provide potential for the application in related oxidative stress-induced diseases.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Characterization of CDs, ZIF-8, and CDs@ZIF-8. (a) TEM image and particle size distribution diagram of CDs (inset is HRTEM image). (b, c) SEM images and particle size distribution diagrams of ZIF-8 (b) and CDs@ZIF-8 (c) (inset is TEM image). (d) Elemental mapping images of CDs@ZIF-8. XRD spectra (e), FTIR spectra (f) of CDs, ZIF-8, and CDs@ZIF-8 NPs. (g) Photographs of ZIF-8 and CDs@ZIF-8 powders. (h) Fluorescence spectra of CDs, ZIF-8, and CDs@ZIF-8 NPs (Ex = 380 nm, inset are photographs of CDs, ZIF-8, and CDs@ZIF-8 under UV light, Ex = 365 nm). (i) Linear fit curve of fluorescence intensity and CDs concentration (n = 3). (j) XPS full survey spectra of CDs, ZIF-8, and CDs@ZIF-8. The high-resolution spectra of CDs, ZIF-8, and CDs@ZIF-8 for (K) Zn 2p, (L) C 1 s, (M) N 1 s.

ROS scavenging properties by CDs, ZIF-8, and CDs@ZIF-8 nanomaterials in vitro. (a-f) UV − vis absorption spectra of KMnO4 (a), ABTS•+ (b), DPPH• (c), •O2 – (d), •OH (e) and H2O2 (f) exposed to CDs (25 μg/mL), ZIF-8 (25 μg/mL), CDs@ZIF-8–25 (25 μg/mL), and CDs@ZIF-8–50 (50 μg/mL), respectively. (g, h) EPR spectra analysis of the •O2 – (g) and •OH (h) scavenging by CDs (25 μg/mL), ZIF-8 (25 μg/mL), CDs@ZIF-8–25 (25 μg/mL) and CDs@ZIF-8–50 (50 μg/mL). (i) Schematic illustration of ROS scavenging process by CDs@ZIF-8 NPs.

Biocompatibility of CDs, ZIF-8, and CDs@ZIF-8. (a-c) Viability of HK-2 cells cultured with different concentrations of CDs (a), ZIF-8 (b), and CDs@ZIF-8 (c) (n = 6). (d-f) Absorbance at 450 nm of CD-treated (d), ZIF-8-treated (e), or CDs@ZIF-8 treated (f) HK-2 cells after incubation with CCK-8 (Concentration of 50 μg/mL, n = 6). (g-i) Hemolysis ratio of red cells cultured with different concentrations of CDs (g), ZIF-8 (h), and CDs@ZIF-8 (i) (insert are images of NPs after 1 h of incubation with red blood cell solution) (n = 6).

The scavenging effect of CDs@ZIF-8 on H2O2, •OH, and •O2 – in HK-2 cells. Fluorescence microscopy images of HK-2 cells in the negative control group (no ROS or CDs@ZIF-8), positive control group (with three ROS), and experimental groups (with ROS and CDs@ZIF-8).

Protection of CDs@ZIF-8 to HK-2 cells against ROS–induced oxidative damage. (a-c) Cell viability of HK-2 cells treated with CDs, ZIF-8, and CDs@ZIF-8 nanomaterials for 3 h followed by treatment with 400 μM H2O2 (a), 80/800 μM Fe2+/H2O2 (b), or 1 μM rotenone (c) for 12 h (n = 6). Significant difference between groups is indicated at *P < 0.05, **P < 0.01, and ***P < 0.001 levels. (d) Fluorescence microscopy images of live/dead (green/red) staining of HK-2 cells treated with CDs@ZIF-8 with 400 μM H2O2 for 12 h. (e) Morphology of HK-2 cells treated with CDs@ZIF-8 (50 μg/mL) with or without 400 μM H2O2 for 12 h.

Mechanism of the antioxidant effect of CDs@ZIF-8. (a-d) Expression levels of MDA (a), SOD (b), GPx (c), and GSH (d) in the HK-2 cells of different treatment groups (n = 6). (e) Total protein and nuclear protein of HK-2 cells were analyzed by western blot with specific antibodies (Nrf2, Lamin B1, HO-1, and GAPDH). (f-h) Inflammatory factors of IL-6 (f), IL-1β (g), and TNF-α (h) in different treatment groups (n = 6). Significant difference between treatment and control groups is indicated at *P < 0.05, **P < 0.01, and ***P < 0.001 levels.
Schematic illustration of the synthesis of CDs@ZIF-8 and its ROS-scavenging to protect HK-2 cells.

head.ce:title: Carbon dots encapsulated zeolitic imidazolate framework-8 as an enhanced multi-antioxidant for efficient cytoprotection to HK-2 cells

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Excessive reactive oxygen species (ROS) can lead to the imbalance of antioxidant system in the body and cause oxidative damage to cells. It is imperative to rationally design nanomaterials with high catalytic activity and multiple antioxidant activities. Here, line peppers-derived carbon dots (CDs) is encapsulated into zeolitic imidazolate framework-8 (CDs@ZIF-8) to achieve enhanced antioxidant activities for improved protective effect on cells. This nanosystem has a broad spectrum of antioxidant properties, which can effectively remove a variety of intracellular ROS and protect cells from ROS-induced death and cytoskeleton damage. In addition, CDs@ZIF-8 can reduce malondialdehyde (MDA) level and increase the enzyme activities of superoxide dismutase (SOD) and glutathione peroxidase (GPx), as well as the level of glutathione (GSH) in human kidney proximal tubular epithelial cells (HK-2) cells. Mechanism studies demonstrated that CDs@ZIF-8 can up-regulate the expression of nuclear factor erythroid 2-related factor 2 (Nrf2) and heme oxygenase-1 (HO-1), allowing the regulation of antioxidant enzymes to further achieve antioxidant effect. Besides, CDs@ZIF-8 inhibited the secretion of proinflammatory cytokines. This work demonstrates that the constructed CDs@ZIF-8 with multi-antioxidant activity can act as a highly efficient intracellular ROS scavenger and provide potential for the application in related oxidative stress-induced diseases.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Reactive oxygen species (ROS), including superoxide radical (•O2 –), hydroxyl radical (•OH), and hydrogen peroxide (H2O2), are generated during cellular respiration and metabolism of organisms [1]. The normal concentration of ROS is an indispensable active substance for cell life activities, which participates in a series of biological reactions in the organism such as various enzyme reactions, electron transfer of mitochondria, signal transduction, gene expression, and antibacterial activity of neutrophils and macrophages [2–5]. However, abnormal concentrations of ROS can destroy the redox homeostasis in the organism, produce oxidative stress, damage the DNA and proteins of cells [6,7].

Nanotechnology-mediated antioxidant therapy has been shown to be a promising approach for treating diseases caused by oxidative damage [8–12]. So far, some nanomaterials such as fullerenes [13], carbon nanotubes [14], graphene [15], and nanocomposites [16] have been extensively used in antioxidant studies. Among these nanomaterials, carbon dots (CDs) have attracted much interest due to their high antioxidant activity (large surface-to-volume ratio) and various ROS scavenging capacity (tunable structure) [17]. CDs are an emerging class of carbon nanomaterials with particle size generally less than 10 nm [18], they have been widely used in bio-detection [19], drug delivery [20–22], and bioimaging [23] due to their high fluorescence activity, variety, and good biocompatibility [24]. More and more CDs-based materials with excellent properties have been synthesized for antioxidant research [25,26]. For example, CDs synthesized with gynostemma as a precursor exhibited excellent antioxidant activity and enhanced the expression of relevant antioxidant genes in zebrafish [25]. One-pot microwave hydrothermal synthesis of Mn, N, and S-doped CDs has been reported to be applied for bioimaging and ROS scavenging as a multifunctional therapeutic platform [26].

Metal-organic Frameworks (MOFs) are porous crystalline materials composed of organic ligands and inorganic metal ions or metal clusters [27]. Due to their ultra-high porosity and specific surface area, adjustable pore size, and easy to design diverse structures, MOFs are considered to have broad application prospects in the biological field, especially as a carrier for materials [28]. Zeolitic imidazolate framework-8 (ZIF-8) is an acid-responsive MOF formed by the coordination of Zn2+ and the N atom on the 2-methylimidazole (2-Mim) ring, and has been shown to react with H2O2 and possess certain antioxidant function [29]. Moreover, ZIF-8 encapsulation can change the surface properties of nanoparticles (NPs) and prevent their aggregation, thereby improving their intrinsic properties under physiological conditions [30].

Inspired by this, we hope to use ZIF-8 to encapsulate CDs to achieve multiple antioxidant effect and protect cells from ROS-induced damage. In this work, CDs with good antioxidant effects was prepared from cheap and readily available line peppers. Then, CDs encapsulated ZIF-8 NPs (CDs@ZIF-8 NPs) was synthesized in situ, which showed enhanced multi-antioxidant activities compared with the CDs alone. As expected, CDs@ZIF-8 exhibited excellent reducing ability and free radical (ABTS [2,2′-azinobis-(3-ethylbenzothiazoline-6-sulfonate)] free radical (ABTS•+), 1,1-diphenyl-2-picrylhydrazyl [DPPH•], •O2 –, •OH, and H2O2) scavenging ability. In addition, the negligible cytotoxicity and good biocompatibility of CDs@ZIF-8 NPs make their antioxidant application in cells possible. Next, a series of experiments showed that CDs@ZIF-8 NPs can effectively eliminate intracellular ROS, including H2O2, •OH, and •O2 –. In the cytoprotection assay, CDs@ZIF-8 was more effective than CDs and ZIF-8 in preventing the three ROS-induced reduction in cell viability, demonstrating the enhanced activity. In addition, CDs@ZIF-8 NPs could attenuate ROS-induced cell death and cell morphological damage (H2O2 was taken as an example in this work). The results of oxidative stress related indicators showed that CDs@ZIF-8 NPs could reduce the level of malondialdehyde (MDA) in cells, increase the activity of antioxidant enzymes (superoxide dismutase [SOD] and glutathione peroxidase [GPx]) and glutathione (GSH) level in cells, and inhibit the expression of inflammatory factors (interleukin 6 [IL-6], interleukin 1β [IL-1β], and tumor necrosis factor α [TNF-α]). More importantly, we found that CDs@ZIF-8 upregulated the expression of nuclear factor erythroid 2-related factor 2 (Nrf2) and heme oxygenase-1 (HO-1), revealing the mechanism by which CDs@ZIF-8 exerted antioxidant protective effects on cells. The synthesis method of CDs@ZIF-8 and its effects on ROS-stimulated HK-2 cells were described in Scheme 1 . In summary, this work provides a synergistic nano-antioxidant material with ZIF-8 as the bioactive surface-modified shell and CDs as the functional core. CDs@ZIF-8 effectively protects cells from ROS-induced damage, which will provide a beneficial strategy for the development of novel antioxidants to cope with ROS-induced diseases.

Line peppers was purchased from supermarket (Zhengzhou, China). Potassium permanganate (KMnO4), ABTS, potassium persulfate (K2S2O8), DPPH•, β-NADH, phenazine methosulfate (PMS), nitrotetrazolium blue chloride (NBT), ferrous sulfate heptahydrate (FeSO4·7H2O), 30 % H2O2, 3,3′,5,5′-etramethylbenzidine (TMB), titanic sulfate [Ti(SO4)2], xanthine, xanthine oxidase, 5,5-dimethyl-1-pyrroline N-oxide (DMPO), Zn(CH3COO)2·2H2O, 2-Mim and rotenone were all analytical reagents from Aladdin. Dulbecco’s modified Eagle’s medium (DMEM) and fetal bovine serum (FBS) were purchased from Gibco. CCK-8 assay kit, 4′,6-diamidino-2-phenylindole (DAPI), 4 % paraformaldehyde, anti-GAPDH monoclonal antibody, goat anti-mouse IgG/HRP, and goat anti-rabbit IgG/HRP were purchased from Solarbio. Dihydroethidium (DHE), actin-tracker red-rhodamine, ROS assay kit, Calcein/PI live/dead assay kit, MDA assay kit, SOD assay kit, GPx assay kit, GSH assay kit, nuclear and cytoplasmic protein extraction kit, and ECL chemiluminescence kit were purchased from Beyotime. All inflammatory factor-linked immunosorbent assay (ELISA) kits were purchased from Jianglai Biological Company (Shanghai, China). HK-2 cells were purchased from Jinyuan Biotechnology Company (Shanghai, China). Anti-Nrf2 rabbit polyclonal antibody and anti-HO-1 rabbit polyclonal antibody were purchased from Proteintech (Wuhan, China). Anti-Lamin B1 recombinant rabbit monoclonal antibody was purchased from HUABIO Company (Zhejiang, China).

The synthesized nanomaterials were characterized by different analysis. Briefly, Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images were acquired with a FEI TECNAIG2F20-S-TWIN electron microscope. Scanning electron microscope (SEM) images were acquired with a ZEISS Gemini SEM 300 electron microscope. X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns were measured using an X-ray diffractometer (PANalytical, X’Pert PRO). Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) was performed on a Nexus 470 (Thermo Fisher) spectrometer. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) measurements used a Thermo Fisher ESCALAB 250Xi surface analysis system. The ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis) absorption spectra were measured using Perse TU-1810PC spectrophotometer. The fluorescence spectra were measured using FLS1000 spectrofluorometer. The size distribution was detected by Zeta sizer Nano ZS. Electron paramagnetic resonance (EPR) spectra were acquired with a EPR spectrometer (EMX-9.5/12). The element content in the liquid was determined by ICP-OES/MS (Agilent 5110 OES, USA). Cell fluorescence pictures were obtained by fluorescence microscope (Zeiss, Jena, Germany). Immunoblots of target proteins were obtained by BIO-RAD exposure apparatus. All characterizations were taken at room temperature.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of CDs, ZIF-8, and CDs@ZIF-8
ce:section.ce:para: For CDs, first, 25 g of chopped pepper was placed in 40 mL of deionized water. Then, the mixture was placed into a reaction kettle and the reaction was carried out at 180 °C for 6 h. The resulting mixture was filtered through a 0.22 μm filter membrane to remove insoluble impurities, followed by dialysis for 24 h to remove the unreacted fraction, and then lyophilized to obtain a solid powder and stored at 4 °C.

ZIF-8 was synthesized as previously reported [31]. Briefly, 0.0053 g, 0.0105 g, 0.21 g, 0.42 g, and 0.84 g of 2-Mim were respectively dissolved with 0.028 g of Zn(CH3COO)2·2H2O in 15 mL of methanol and left for 2 h at room temperature. Next, the mixtures were centrifuged at 12,000 rpm for 10 min and washed three times with methanol. Finally, these products were placed in a vacuum oven at 70 °C overnight to obtain ZIF-8-a, ZIF-8-b, ZIF-8-c, ZIF-8-d, ZIF-8-e, respectively.

For CDs@ZIF-8, 1.5 g, 3 g, 6 g, 12 g, 24 g of CDs and 0.168 g of Zn(CH3COO)2·2H2O were dissolved in 15 mL of methanol and stirred at room temperature for 60 min, then 0.252 g of 2-Mim was added and stirred for 30 min. Next, the mixtures were treated in the same way as for the preparation of ZIF-8 to obtain CDs@ZIF-8–1, CDs@ZIF-8–2, CDs@ZIF-8–3, CDs@ZIF-8–4, CDs@ZIF-8–5.

The reducing capacity of the material can be judged according to the reduction degree of KMnO4 [32]. CDs (25 μg/mL), ZIF-8 (25 μg/mL), CDs@ZIF-8–25 (25 μg/mL), and CDs@ZIF-8–50 (50 μg/mL) were added to KMnO4 solution (200 μM), and pure water was used as the control group. The UV–vis absorption spectra of the mixtures at 400 nm-700 nm were measured. The reducing capacity was evaluated by the absorbance of the mixtures at 525 nm. The reducing capacity of the material was calculated by the following formula, in which K0 is the absorbance of the control group at 525 nm and KS is the absorbance of the mixture of the material and KMnO4.
Reducing capacity = (K0-KS)/K0 × 100 %

ABTS•+ is obtained by the reaction of ABTS with K2S2O8 over 3 h [33]. CDs (25 μg/mL), ZIF-8 (25 μg/mL), CDs@ZIF-8–25 (25 μg/mL), and CDs@ZIF-8–50 (50 μg/mL) were added to ABTS•+ solution (280 μM), and pure water was used as the control group. The UV–vis absorption spectra of the mixtures at 500 nm-900 nm were measured. ABTS•+ scavenging activity was evaluated by the absorbance of the mixtures at 732 nm. The scavenging efficiency of the material on ABTS•+ is calculated by the following formula, in which A0 is the absorbance of the control group at 732 nm, and AS is the absorbance of the mixture of the material and ABTS•+ at 732 nm.
ABTS•+ elimination efficiency = (A0-AS)/A0 ×100%

DPPH• is a very stable free radical. The ethanol solution of DPPH• has an absorption at 550 nm [32]. CDs (25 μg/mL), ZIF-8 (25 μg/mL), CDs@ZIF-8–25 (25 μg/mL), and CDs@ZIF-8–50 (50 μg/mL) were added to DPPH• ethanol solution (31.5 μM), and pure water was used as the control group. The UV–vis absorption spectra of the mixtures at 400 nm-700 nm were measured. DPPH• scavenging activity was evaluated by the absorbance of the mixtures at 550 nm. The scavenging efficiency of the material on DPPH• is calculated by the following formula, in which D0 is the absorbance of the control group at 550 nm, and DS is the absorbance of the mixture of the material and DPPH• at 550 nm.
DPPH• elimination efficiency = (D0-DS)/D0 ×100%

β-NADH/PMS/NBT was used as the •O2 – generation system, the •O2 – scavengers can reduce the blue color of NBT [34]. The material and β-NADH (963 μM), NBT (336 μM) and PMS (163 μM) were mixed in order (the solvent was PBS, pH = 7.4), the control group was replaced by pure water. Then, the UV–vis absorption spectra of the mixtures at 400 nm-700 nm were measured after accurate reaction for 5 min. •O2 – scavenging activity was evaluated by the absorbance of the mixtures at 605 nm. The scavenging efficiency of the material on •O2 – is calculated by the following formula, in which S0 is the absorbance of the control group at 605 nm, and SS is the absorbance of the mixture of the material and •O2 – at 605 nm.
•O2 - elimination efficiency = (S0-SS)/S0 ×100%

•OH was generated through Fenton reaction with Fe2+/H2O2 system. TMB solution mixed with •OH will be oxidized to a blue color, with maximum absorption at about 650 nm [29]. The material and TMB (10 mM in DMSO), FeSO4·7H2O (0.2 mM) and H2O2 (1 mM) were mixed in order. Then, the UV–vis absorption spectra of the mixtures at 500 nm-800 nm were measured. •OH scavenging activity was evaluated by the absorbance of the mixtures at 650 nm. The scavenging efficiency of the material on •OH is calculated by the following formula, in which H0 is the absorbance of the control group at 650 nm, and HS is the absorbance of the mixture of the material and •OH at 650 nm.
•OH elimination efficiency = (H0-HS)/H0 ×100%

H2O2 reacts with Ti(SO4)2 to form a yellow precipitate of peroxy-titanium complex, which is dissolved in strong acid. The yellow color of the solution has a linear relationship with the concentration of H2O2 in a certain range, and the concentration of H2O2 in the system can be determined by detecting the absorbance at 408 nm [35]. Briefly, the nanomaterial, H2O2 (10 mM) and Ti(SO4)2 (312.5 Mm, 25 % H2SO4 as solvent) were mixed sequentially and allowed to stand for 30 min. Next, the UV–vis absorption spectrum of the system was measured, and the scavenging activity of H2O2 was evaluated by the absorbance at 408 nm. The scavenging efficiency of the material on H2O2 is calculated by the following formula, in which O0 is the absorbance of the control group at 408 nm, and OS is the absorbance of the mixture of the material and H2O2 at 408 nm.
H2O2 elimination efficiency = (O0-OS)/O0 ×100%

First, •O2 – was generated by the reaction of xanthine (10 mM) and xanthine oxidase (1 U/mL) for 10 min, PBS (pH = 7.4) was used as a solvent. •OH was generated by Fenton reaction using a Fe2+/H2O2 system of FeSO4 (0.2 Mm) and H2O2 (1 mM). Then, the •O2 – and •OH scavenging abilities of CDs, ZIF-8, and CDs@ZIF-8 were examined by EPR spectra analysis: solutions of CDs (25 μg/mL), ZIF-8 (25 μg/mL), CDs@ZIF-8–25 (25 μg/mL), or CDs@ZIF-8–50 (50 μg/mL) were mixed with •O2 – or •OH solutions. Then, DMPO was added to the mixed solution to capture free radicals, and the ability of the material to remove •O2 – and •OH was evaluated by the amplitude intensity of the EPR spectrum [36].
HK-2 cells were incubated in DMEM with 10 % FBS, 100 U/mL penicillin, 50 U/mL streptomycin at 37 °C in a 5 % CO2 incubator.
Cytotoxicity, cell proliferation, and the protective effect of the materials against ROS-induced cell damage were all assessed by CCK-8 assay [37].

HK-2 cells (5 × 103 cells per well) were seeded in 96-well plates. After 24 h of cell growth, the three nanomaterials (12.5 μg/mL, 25 μg/mL, 50 μg/mL, 100 μg/mL) were added to each group, and the cells were incubated for 24 h. The cell viability was measured by CKK-8 assay kit. To examine the effect of the material on cell proliferation, cells were treated with 50 μg/mL material and cell viability was measured daily for 4 consecutive days using a CCK-8 kit.

To select the appropriate ROS stimulation concentration, HK-2 cells (5 × 103 cells per well) were seeded in 96-well plates. After 24 h of cell growth, H2O2 (0 μM, 100 μM, 200 μM, 400 μM, 800 μM, 1600 μM), Fe2+/H2O2 (0/0 μM, 10/100 μM, 20/200 μM, 40/400 μM, 80/800 μM, 160/1600 μM), and rotenone (0 μM, 0.125 μM, 0.25 μM, 0.5 μM, 1 μM, 2 μM) were added to each well and incubated for 12 h, and cell viability was determined by CKK-8 assay.

To test the protective effect of the materials on ROS-stimulated cells, HK-2 cells (5 × 103 cells per well) were seeded in 96-well plates. After 24 h of cell growth, the three nanomaterials (0 μg/mL, 12.5 μg/mL, 50 μg/mL) were added to each well for 3 h and H2O2 (400 μM), Fe2+/H2O2 (80 μM /800 μM), or rotenone (1 μM) were added for 12 h, and cell viability was measured by CKK-8 assay.

To evaluate the biocompatibility of the materials, hemolysis tests were performed. Specifically, the nanomaterials of 12.5 μg/mL, 25 μg/mL, 50 μg/mL, 100 μg/mL, 200 μg/mL were incubated with 5 % red blood cell solution for 1 h and centrifuged at 2000 rpm for 5 min at room temperature. The absorbance of the supernatant was measured at 540 nm [38]. The hemolysis ratio was calculated using the following formula, where R0, R1, and RS are the absorbance at 540 nm in the negative control group, positive control group, and experimental group, respectively.
Hemolysis ratio = (RS-R0)/ (R1-R0) ×100%

Briefly, HK-2 cells (5 × 104 cells per well) were seeded in 24-well plates. After 24 h of cell growth, CDs@ZIF-8 (0 μg/mL, 12.5 μg/mL, 25 μg/mL, 50 μg/mL) were added to each well for 3 h and H2O2 (400 μM), Fe2+/H2O2 (80 μM /800 μM), or rotenone (1 μM) were added for 12 h. There was no nanomaterial in the blank control group and other conditions were the same as in the experimental groups. Next, the cells were incubated with DCFH-DA (10 μM) or DHE (10 μM) for 30min. The intracellular ROS level was observed by the fluorescence intensity of the cells under a fluorescence microscope.

Live/Dead cell staining was executed by the Calcein/PI live/dead assay kit. Briefly, HK-2 cells (5 × 104 cells per well) were seeded in 24-well plates. After 24 h of cell growth, CDs@ZIF-8 (0 μg/mL, 12.5 μg/mL, 25 μg/mL, 50 μg/mL) were added to each well for 3 h and H2O2 (400 μM) were added for 12 h. There was no nanomaterial in the blank control group and other conditions were the same as in the experimental groups. Subsequently, the cells were stained with calcein acetoxymethyl ester (Calcein AM, 1 μM, green, for staining of live cells) and propidium iodide (PI, 10 μg/mL, red, for staining of dead and dying cells) for 30 min. Live and dead cells were observed by fluorescence microscope.

Cell morphology was observed by staining with actin-tracker red-rhodamine and DAPI. Briefly, HK-2 cells (5 × 104 cells per well) were seeded in 24-well plates. After 24 h of cell growth, CDs@ZIF-8 (0 μg/mL, 12.5 μg/mL, 25 μg/mL, 50 μg/mL) were added to each well for 3 h and H2O2 (400 μM) were added for 12 h. There was no nanomaterial in the blank control group and other conditions were the same as in the experimental groups. Subsequently, the cells were fixed with 4 % paraformaldehyde for 20 min. Then, the cells were stained with actin-tracker red-rhodamine for 30 min and counterstained with 1 μg/mL DAPI for 10 min. The cells were observed by fluorescence microscope.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of MDA, SOD, GPx, GSH, and inflammatory factors in cells
ce:section.ce:para: The contents of MDA, SOD, GPx, and GSH in the cells were examined using the kits. Briefly, HK-2 cells (100 × 104 cells per well) were seeded in 6-well plates. After 24 h of cell growth, CDs@ZIF-8 (0 μg/mL, 12.5 μg/mL, 25 μg/mL, 50 μg/mL) were added to each well for 3 h and H2O2 (400 μM) were added for 12 h. There was no nanomaterial in the blank control group and other conditions were the same as in the experimental groups. Subsequently, the medium was removed, the cells were washed twice with PBS, and lysate was added to lyse the cells for 10 min. Next, the cells were scraped off with a cell scraper, centrifuged at 11000 rpm for 20 min at 4 °C, and the supernatant was removed for determination of protein concentration. The levels of MDA, SOD, GPx, and GSH in each group were detected according to the instructions. ELISA was used to detect the levels of inflammatory factors (interleukin 6 [IL-6], interleukin 1β [IL-1β], and tumor necrosis factor α [TNF-α]) in the supernatant of each group.

First, total proteins were extracted from HK-2 cells by lysis and centrifugation, and nuclear proteins were extracted from HK-2 cells using a nuclear and cytoplasmic protein extraction kit. Then, cell extracts were detected by means of sodium dodecyl sulfate–polyacrylamide gel electrophoresis. Subsequently, the gels were electroblotted onto a nitrocellulose membrane. The blots containing total proteins were treated with the anti-HO-1 antiboy or anti-GAPDH antiboy, while the blots containing nuclear proteins were treated with the anti-Nrf2 antiboy or anti-Lamin B1 antiboy. Then, goat anti-rabbit or goat anti-mouse IgG-HRP conjugated secondary antibody was incubated with the membranes. The expression of Nrf2 and HO-1 was detected by chemiluminescence.

All data sets were analyzed by using Origin 9 software and were expressed as means ± SD. One-way analysis of variance (ANOVA) was used for these comparisons. The level of significance was set at probabilities of *P < 0.05 and **P < 0.01 and ***P < 0.001.

In this study, we have synthesized CDs@ZIF-8 NPs to achieve enhanced antioxidant activities. First, CDs were successfully synthesized through a facile hydrothermal method with a size of ∼ 4.5 nm (Fig. 1 a). The corresponding high resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) image (the inset of Fig. 1a) shows 0.21 nm interplanar spacing, which is ascribed to the (100) facet of graphitic carbon [39].

Then, the optimal molar ratio of Zn2+ and 2-Mim for the preparation of ZIF-8 was explored (Table S1). As shown in Fig. S1a, there was significant precipitation in the ZIF-8-c system. However, the methanol solution of stable ZIF-8 should be a white colloidal solution, as shown in the ZIF-8-d system [40]. Moreover, the characteristic peak of the XRD spectrum of ZIF-8-d is sharper than that of ZIF-8-c (Fig. S1b), indicating that the crystallinity of ZIF-8-d is higher [41,42]. Therefore, ZIF-8-d was chosen for the subsequent studies, and the ratio of Zn2+ and 2-Mim in the CDs@ZIF-8 system was kept constant.

Next, in order to allow ZIF-8 to encapsulate a higher content of CDs while ensuring a regular crystal structure of CDs@ZIF-8, the masses of Zn2+ and 2-Mim were fixed, and the mass of CDs introduced was changed to obtain five CDs@ZIF-8 NPs with different CDs content. They are denoted as CDs@ZIF-8–1, CDs@ZIF-8–2, CDs@ZIF-8–3, CDs@ZIF-8–4, CDs@ZIF-8–5 (Table S2). The XRD spectra showed that the crystallinity of CDs@ZIF-8 decreased with the increase of CDs incorporation (Fig. S2a). Furthermore, SEM images showed that ZIF-8, CDs@ZIF-8–1, CDs@ZIF-8–2, and CDs@ZIF-8–3 presented regular dodecahedral structures (Fig. S2b-e). CDs@ZIF-8–4 were intercalated structures (Fig. S2f), and CDs@ZIF-8–5 clearly lost their crystal structure (Fig. S2g). Therefore, CDs@ZIF-8–3 was selected for subsequent experiments based on these results. (For ease of presentation, in the following section, CDs@ZIF-8 refers to CDs@ZIF-8–3 in the absence of special circumstances).

TEM and SEM images showed that the synthesized ZIF-8 and CDs@ZIF-8 NPs behaved as highly monodisperse particles. The average particle sizes of ZIF-8 and CDs@ZIF-8 were ∼ 125 and ∼ 308 nm, respectively (Fig. 1b, c). The element mapping images of CDs@ZIF-8 showed that the trace element Fe contained in CDs (the precursor line peppers contain Fe element) and the Zn contained in ZIF-8 both appeared in CDs@ZIF-8 (Fig. 1d), confirming the successful synthesis of CDs@ZIF-8. After the introduction of CDs into ZIF-8, the XRD pattern of CDs@ZIF-8 almost has no obvious change, indicating that the introduction of CDs had no effect on the crystal integrity of ZIF-8 (Fig. 1e). However, due to the relatively low XRD signal intensity of CDs, the characteristic peak of CDs at 21° is difficult to observe in the XRD pattern of CDs@ZIF-8 [43,44]. The FTIR spectra of CDs, ZIF-8, CDs@ZIF-8 are shown in Fig. 1f, the strong absorption peaks of –OH in CDs and CDs@ZIF-8 were observed at ∼ 3400 cm−1 [45]. For CDs@ZIF-8, the absorption peaks at 3134 cm−1 and 2927 cm−1 correspond to the stretching vibration of the CH bond in the imidazole ring and the methyl group, respectively. The peak at 1585 cm−1 is the stretching vibration of the CN bond. The absorption peaks at 1145 cm−1 and 995 cm−1 are attributed to the stretching vibration of the CN bond [44]. Similarly, these characteristic peaks were all found in ZIF-8. In addition, the ZIF-8 powder appears white, while the CDs@ZIF-8 powder appears brown, which may be caused by the incorporation of brown CDs into ZIF-8 (Fig. 1g). The excitation and emission spectra of CDs are shown in Fig. S3a (ex = 380 nm, em = 483 nm). The fluorescence emission spectra of ZIF-8 and CDs@ZIF-8 solutions were detected (Fig. 1h), and it was found that the fluorescence peaks of CDs and CDs@ZIF-8 were located at 483 nm, while the fluorescence peak of ZIF-8 was not there. Under UV light, CDs and CDs@ZIF-8 solutions showed blue fluorescence, while ZIF-8 solution showed no obvious fluorescence, which also indicated that CDs was successfully encapsulated in ZIF-8. Furthermore, based on the relationship between the fluorescence intensity and the concentration of CDs (in the low concentration range of CDs, Fig. 1i), the loading of CDs in CDs@ZIF-8 was calculated to be 21.1 %. Nevertheless, the characteristic UV–vis absorption peak (280 nm) of CDs was not observed in CDs@ZIF-8 (Fig. S3b) [46], which may be due to the high relative content of ZIF-8 in the system masking the characteristic absorption peak of CDs. To confirm this, three groups that contain different contents of ZIF-8 and CDs were conducted. As shown in Fig. S3c, no characteristic peak of CDs was found when high content of ZIF-8 (H-ZIF-8) and low content of CDs (l-CDs) were mixed physically, while the peaks appeared and the absorbance also increased along with the enlarged relative content of CDs. This phenomenon affirmed the above conjecture. The chemical composition and electronic structure of CDs, ZIF-8, and CDs@ZIF-8 were analyzed by XPS (Fig. 1j). The peaks of the Zn 2p spectrum of CDs@ZIF-8 are located at 1021.5 eV and 1044.8 eV, corresponding to two different types of peaks, 2p 3/2 and 2p 1/2 [47], which is consistent with the peak positions of ZIF-8 (Fig. 1k), indicating that the main support structure of ZIF-8 remains after the incorporation of CDs and the CDs may be physically loaded into ZIF-8. In the C 1 s spectrum, the characteristic peaks of CDs@ZIF-8 at 284.8 eV, 286.0 eV, and 288.5 eV correspond to CC, CN, and CO, respectively (Fig. 1l). In the N 1s spectrum, the characteristic peaks of CDs@ZIF-8 at 398.8 eV, 399.7 eV, and 400.4 eV correspond to CN, N-(CO)-, and CN, respectively (Fig. 1m) [44]. These peaks basically match the peak positions of CDs and ZIF-8, further confirming the successful synthesis of CDs@ZIF-8.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Enhancement of antioxidant activity by ZIF-8 encapsulation
ce:section.ce:para: In this study, we have evaluated the antioxidant activity of CDs (25 μg/mL), ZIF-8 (25 μg/mL), CDs@ZIF-8–25 (25 μg/mL), and CDs@ZIF-8–50 (50 μg/mL). First, the reducing capacity of the materials to KMnO4 was evaluated. As shown in Fig. 2 a, CDs@ZIF-8 has a stronger reducing ability than CDs and ZIF-8. The reducing capacity of CDs@ZIF-8–50 was 65.0 %, which was much higher than that of CDs, ZIF-8, and CDs@ZIF-8–25 (28.3 %, 17.7 % and 39.7 %, respectively). To explore the free radical scavenging activity of the materials, ABTS•+ and DPPH• are typically used as indicators to assess the total antioxidant activity of antioxidants in vitro.As shown in Fig. 2b and c, the clearance rate of CDs@ZIF-8–25 was 59.8 % for ABTS•+ and 47.9 % for DPPH•, which was higher than that of CDs (53.9 % for ABTS•+ and 29.6 % for DPPH•), ZIF-8 (42.7 % for ABTS•+and 16.0 % for DPPH•). The clearance rate of CDs@ZIF-8–50 was 81.2 % for ABTS•+ and 50.3 % for DPPH•.

Successively, the scavenging effect of the materials on three important ROS (•O2 –, •OH, and H2O2) was evaluated. As shown in Fig. 2d-f, the clearance rates of •O2 –, •OH, and H2O2 by CDs@ZIF-8–50 were 57.9 %, 55.8 %, and 46.4 %, respectively, which were higher than that of CDs (20.8 % for •O2 –, 30.5 % for •OH, and 3.2 % for H2O2), ZIF-8 (5.6 % for •O2 –, 11.2 % for •OH, and 17.6 % for H2O2), and CDs@ZIF-8–25 (34.4 % for •O2 –, 34.5 % for •OH, and 24.2 % for H2O2). EPR spectra were used to examine the •O2 – and •OH scavenging ability of CDs, ZIF-8, and CDs@ZIF-8. •O2 – was generated by the reaction of xanthine and xanthine oxidase, •OH was generated by the Fenton reaction of the Fe2+/H2O2 system. DMPO was used to trap these two free radicals to produce a specific signal. As shown in Fig. 2g, the EPR spectrum of the xanthine/xanthine oxidase reaction showed a specific signal for the DMPO-•OOH adduct, indicating successful •O2 – generation. When CDs, ZIF-8, CDs@ZIF-8–25, and CDs@ZIF-8–50 were added to the system, the signal intensity decreased, especially for CDs@ZIF-8–50. Moreover, the three NPs also reduced the EPR amplitude of DMPO-•OH, especially for CDs@ZIF-8–50 (Fig. 2h). These results suggest that ZIF-8 encapsulation can improve the antioxidant activity of CDs, and the radical scavenging efficiency of CDs@ZIF-8 increases in a concentration dependent manner.

Subsequently, the possible processes by which CDs@ZIF-8 eliminates H2O2, •O2 –, and •OH were explored. Firstly, CDs@ZIF-8 was dissolved in 5 % H2O2 solution, left to react for 3 h, and then TEM images were taken. As shown in Fig. S5a, the crystalline structure of the initial ZIF-8 disappeared, indicating the collapse of CDs@ZIF-8, which is consistent with a previous report [29]. Then, the reaction solution was centrifuged, and the supernatant was taken for Inductively Coupled Plasma (ICP) test and fluorescence spectroscopy test. The ICP results indicated that Zn2+ was present in the supernatant at a concentration of 31.2 mg/L. In addition, CDs’ characteristic fluorescence peak (483 nm) appeared in the fluorescence spectrum of the supernatant (Fig. S5b), which proved that CDs was released from ZIF-8 after its disintegration and further scavenged ROS. Fig. 2i is a schematic representation of the ROS scavenging process by CDs@ZIF-8. The above results indicate that the CDs@ZIF-8 nanosystem can control the release of CDs under oxidative stress, which makes it more suitable for biological applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Biocompatibility of CDs, ZIF-8, and CDs@ZIF-8
ce:section.ce:para: Before applying CDs@ZIF-8 to HK-2 cells, the viability of HK-2 cells cultured with different concentrations of CDs, ZIF-8, and CDs@ZIF-8 were determined. As shown in Fig. 3 a-c, the viability of HK-2 cells cultured with CD, ZIF-8, or CDs@ZIF-8 at concentrations of 0 to 100 μg/mL for 24 h were all above 85 %, indicating that these three NPs have low cytotoxicity. In addition, the cells proliferated normally in the medium containing the three NPs (50 μg/mL), and the proliferation rate was fastest on day 2 and then leveled off (Fig. 3d-f). The trend of cell proliferation was basically the same as that of the control group (Fig. S4), indicating that the three NPs had little interference on cell proliferation. Then, the hemolysis ratio of CDs, ZIF-8, and CDs@ZIF-8 at concentrations of 0 to 200 μg/mL was detected. As shown in Fig. 3g-i, erythrocytes in the positive control group were ruptured and hemolysis was evidently observed. However, the groups treated with three NPs and the negative control group did not show obvious hemolysis, and the hemolysis rate was less than 3 %, reflecting their good blood compatibility [38]. These results indicate that these three NPs have low cytotoxicity and good biocompatibility, which can be used in cellular experiments and have the potential to be used in the treatment of ROS-related diseases in vivo.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Scavenging effect of CDs@ZIF-8 on ROS in HK-2 cells
ce:section.ce:para: Under oxidative stress conditions, the most common three kinds of ROS (H2O2, •OH, •O2 –) are produced in cells, which are seriously harmful to organisms [48]. Therefore, the scavenging effect of CDs@ZIF-8 on the three ROS in cells was evaluated. First, the cell injury models induced by H2O2, •OH, or •O2 – were established. The cells were directly cultured in the medium containing H2O2 to establish H2O2-induced cell injury model. •OH-induced cell injury model was obtained by culturing cells in cell medium containing Fe2+ and H2O2 [49]. In addition, owing to that rotenone is a mitochondrial electron transport chain inhibitor that catalyzes superoxide production [50], a model of •O2 –-induced cell damage was established by culturing cells in medium containing rotenone. Next, the optimal concentrations of the three ROS were explored. As shown in Fig. S6a-c, cell viability decreased with increasing H2O2, Fe2+/H2O2, or rotenone concentrations. When HK-2 cells were treated with 400 μM H2O2, 80/800 μM Fe2+/H2O2, and 1 μM rotenone, the cell viability decreased to about 60 %. In addition, qualitative H2O2 and •OH levels in HK-2 cells were examined with DCFH-DA probe, and the •O2 – levels were examined with DHE probe. As shown in Fig. S6d, the green fluorescence intensity of cells treated with 400 μM H2O2, 80/800 μM Fe2+/H2O2 and the red fluorescence intensity of cells treated with 1 μM rotenone were significantly increased compared with the negative control group, indicating that the levels of H2O2, •OH, and •O2 – was up-regulated after stimulation. Therefore, 400 μM H2O2, 80/800 μM Fe2+/H2O2, and 1 μM rotenone were selected as suitable conditions to stimulate cellular ROS production after the consideration of stimulus amount, cell viability and fluorescence image. Next, the ROS scavenging effect of CDs@ZIF-8 on the three cell models was evaluated. As shown in Fig. 4 , in the three cell models containing ROS, the fluorescence intensity decreased with the increase of CDs@ZIF-8 concentration, indicating that CDs@ZIF-8 can effectively reduce the levels of various ROS in cells and may be used to protect against ROS-induced cell damage.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Protective effect of CDs@ZIF-8 against ROS-induced cell damage
ce:section.ce:para: Excessive ROS levels can reduce cell viability or even induce cell death, while CDs@ZIF-8 have been demonstrated to scavenge ROS in cells (Fig. 4). Therefore, CDs@ZIF-8 may possess the protection effect towards cells, thus maintaining the normal cell growth. In order to investigate this, various experiments were conducted against H2O2, •OH, or •O2 –-stimulated HK-2 cells with or without CDs@ZIF-8. As shown in Fig. 5 a-c, 400 μM H2O2, 80/800 μM Fe2+/H2O2, and 1 μM rotenone significantly inhibited the proliferation of HK-2 cells, resulting in a reduction in cell viability. CDs, ZIF-8, and CDs@ZIF-8 NPs could attenuate the cell damage caused by these three ROS. In particular, compared with CDs and ZIF-8 treated groups, the pretreatment of cells with CDs@ZIF-8 NPs could alleviate the cell damage to a greater extent. Especially, HK-2 cell viability maintained above 90 % after pretreated with 50 μg/mL CDs@ZIF-8, demonstrating the efficient cytoprotective effect of CDs@ZIF-8.

To directly visualize this result, live/dead staining (calcein AM and PI staining) were used to detect the survival and death of HK-2 cells. In this experiment, H2O2 was selected as the stimuli model to induce cell death. As shown in Fig. S7, cells treated with different concentrations of CDs@ZIF-8 showed almost no red fluorescence, implying that CDs@ZIF-8 almost did not induce cell death, which is consistent with the experimental results of the effect of CDs@ZIF-8 on cell viability (Fig. 3c). However, cells treated with H2O2 showed significant red fluorescence, which indicated that severe cell death had occurred (Fig. 5d). However, it was found that a significant decrease in the number of dead cells and an increase in the number of living cells in the CDs@ZIF-8 pretreatment group. When the concentration of CDs@ZIF-8 was 50 μg/mL, the survival or death of cells was basically the same as that of the negative control group. The above results indicate that CDs@ZIF-8 can effectively rescue the decrease in cell viability caused by the three ROS, prevent cell death due to excessive ROS, and has good antioxidant and cytoprotective ability.

Moreover, cell death is often accompanied by changes in cell morphology and structure, resulting in cell detachment from contact with surrounding cells and loss of normal cell division regulation ability [51,52]. Therefore, the changes of cell morphology can be used as an indicator of cell malignant transformation. The protective effect of CDs@ZIF-8 against ROS-induced cell damage was explored according to the changes in the cytoskeleton. As shown in Fig. 5e, actin-tracker red-rhodamine was used for staining of the cytoskeleton and DAPI was used to localize the nucleus. It was found that the cytoskeleton was clear and the adhesion between cells was obvious in the control group and CDs@ZIF-8 treated group, which indicated the normal growth state of the cells. However, the microfilaments of the cells stimulated by H2O2 showed damage, unclear cytoskeletal structure, morphological shrinkage, and diffused fluorescence. In contrast, the morphology of HK-2 cells co-cultured with CDs@ZIF-8 and H2O2 was significantly improved, indicating that CDs@ZIF-8 could effectively protect HK-2 cells from H2O2-induced damage and maintain the cytoskeleton.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Protective action mechanisms of CDs@ZIF-8 against ROS-induced injury in HK-2 cells
ce:section.ce:para: The protective action mechanisms of CDs@ZIF-8 against ROS-induced injury in HK-2 cells was further explored. MDA, the product of membrane lipid peroxidation, is usually used to characterize and measure the level of plasma membrane peroxidation [53]. Generally, the higher the MDA level, the deeper of peroxidation extent. Therefore, MDA level in HK-2 cells was firstly examined. It was found that the level of MDA in HK-2 cells treated with CDs@ZIF-8 was much lower than that in H2O2-stimulated cells, indicating that CDs@ZIF-8 was effective in reducing cellular lipid peroxidation (Fig. 6 a).

SOD and GPx are indispensable antioxidant enzymes in the body, and GSH is an important antioxidant. These substances play key roles in scavenging ROS and reducing cell damage caused by ROS in human body [54–57]. Usually, continuous ROS stimulation towards cells leads to the lower expression level of antioxidant enzyme or non-enzymatic molecule (i.e. SOD, GPx and GSH), while the internalization of exogenous ROS scavengers can promote their levels [58–60]. Therefore, the enzymatic activities of SOD, GPx, and the level of GSH in HK-2 cells were further examined to estimate the effect of CDs@ZIF-8. As shown in Fig. 6b-d, the activities of SOD and GPx and the GSH content in HK-2 cells stimulated by H2O2 (12 h) were lower than those in the control group. This may be because excessive ROS will initially lead to an increase in the activity of intracellular antioxidant enzymes as a coping mechanism, but the continuous increase of ROS may eventually lead to the inability to maintain the activity of antioxidant enzymes at a high level. Therefore, although antioxidant enzymes and antioxidants played a role in scavenging part of ROS, the antioxidant capacity of the cell is not sufficient to combat excessive ROS for a long time, which eventually leads to cell damage and affects the level of intracellular antioxidants. However, the levels of SOD, GPx, and GSH were increased in cells treated with CDs@ZIF-8, indicating that the presence of CDs@ZIF-8 could maintain these antioxidant related substances at higher concentrations, thus further eliminating ROS.

Nrf2 is a major transcription factor controlling the antioxidant system and is considered an important molecular target for cytopreventive and protective effects in many reactions [61]. Upon stimulation by endogenous ROS or electrophiles, Nrf2 translocalizes to the nucleus, leading to transcription of antioxidant enzymes that promote cytoprotection [62]. Therefore, in order to further clarify the molecular mechanism of CDs@ZIF-8′s protective effect on oxidative damage cells, the effect of CDs@ZIF-8 on the expression of antioxidant pathway Nrf2 and its downstream protein (HO-1) in oxidative damage HK-2 cells was detected. Lamin B1 was selected as an internal control for nuclear protein and GAPDH as an internal control for total protein. As shown in Fig. 6e, Nrf2 expression in nuclear proteins was decreased in the H2O2-induced group, which is consistent with a previous report [63]. However, there was an increase in nuclear Nrf2 expression in cells treated with CDs@ZIF-8. Therefore, CDs@ZIF-8 may activate Nrf2 to enter the nucleus, and then allow Nrf2 to regulate downstream antioxidant proteins and antioxidant enzymes to achieve antioxidant effect, maintaining cellular redox homeostasis.

In addition, excessive ROS can directly promote the production of proinflammatory cytokines, which can lead to cellular inflammation [57]. Therefore, the effect of CDs@ZIF-8 on the levels of inflammatory factors caused by ROS was examined. As shown in Fig. 6f-h, the secretion of IL-6, IL-1β, and TNF-α was significantly enhanced in HK-2 cells compared with the control group. In the group of cells pretreated with CDs@ZIF-8, the levels of proinflammatory cytokines decreased to varying degrees in a dose-dependent manner. The results suggest that CDs@ZIF-8 can effectively inhibit the increase in the levels of inflammatory factors caused by ROS, thereby reducing further damage to cells caused by inflammation.

In this work, CDs@ZIF-8 nanocomposites were prepared by using pepper CDs with a broad spectrum of ROS scavenging ability as the core and ZIF-8 with H2O2 scavenging ability as the shell. The results showed that ZIF-8 effectively enhanced the antioxidant capacity of CDs. The clearance rates of ABTS•+, DPPH•, •O2 –, •OH and H2O2 by CDs@ZIF-8 (50 μg/mL) were 81.2 %, 50.3 %, 57.9 %, 55.8 % and 46.4 %, more than many antioxidant CDs have been reported [26,64,65]. CDs@ZIF-8 exhibited excellent ROS scavenging ability in HK-2 cells, protecting cells from ROS-induced death and morphological damage. It has been found that CDs@ZIF-8 can not only directly reduce the production of intracellular ROS, but also increase the levels of antioxidant enzymes and antioxidants in HK-2 cells, promote the expression of antioxidant proteins, and reduce the secretion of pro-inflammatory cytokines, thereby protecting cells. This unique composite antioxidant structure provides some guidance for the design of nanomaterials with high antioxidant activity and provides new ideas for the treatment of ROS-related diseases.
